text: a+b=b+a
sum=n(n+1)/2
ax^2+bx+c=0
addition is commutative meaning a plus b equals b plus a
multiplication distributes over addition
the pythagorean theorem relates the sides of a right triangle
prime numbers are divisible only by one and themselves
zero is the additive identity element
one is the multiplicative identity element
negative times negative equals positive
the area of a circle is pi times the radius squared
a function maps each input to exactly one output
the slope of a line is the change in y divided by the change in x
logarithm is the inverse operation of exponentiation
absolute value gives the distance of a number from zero on the number line
the derivative of a function gives the instantaneous rate of change at any point and is defined as the limit of the difference quotient as the interval approaches zero
integration is the reverse of differentiation and computes the area under a curve between two bounds using the fundamental theorem of calculus
the quadratic formula gives the solutions of ax squared plus bx plus c equals zero as x equals negative b plus or minus the square root of b squared minus four ac all divided by two a
a matrix is a rectangular array of numbers and matrix multiplication combines rows of the first matrix with columns of the second to produce a new matrix
the binomial theorem expands the power of a sum into a series of terms involving binomial coefficients
euler's formula states that e to the power of i times theta equals cosine theta plus i times sine theta connecting exponential and trigonometric functions in the complex plane
probability measures the likelihood of an event occurring and ranges from zero meaning impossible to one meaning certain
the chain rule in calculus states that the derivative of a composite function equals the derivative of the outer function times the derivative of the inner function
a vector space is a collection of vectors that can be added together and multiplied by scalars while satisfying specific axioms of closure associativity and distributivity
the central limit theorem states that the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as the sample size increases regardless of the shape of the population distribution

the fundamental theorem of algebra states that every non constant polynomial equation with complex coefficients has at least one complex root and a polynomial of degree n has exactly n roots counted with multiplicity

a group in abstract algebra is a set equipped with an operation that satisfies closure associativity has an identity element and every element has an inverse and groups capture the essence of symmetry in mathematics

the riemann integral partitions an interval into subintervals and approximates the area under a curve by summing rectangles and as the partition becomes finer the sum converges to the exact area if the function is integrable

eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix describe the directions along which a linear transformation acts by scaling without changing direction and they are fundamental to understanding dynamical systems quantum mechanics and principal component analysis

taylors theorem states that any sufficiently smooth function can be approximated near a point by a polynomial whose coefficients are determined by the derivatives of the function at that point and the accuracy improves as more terms are included

the divergence theorem relates the flux of a vector field through a closed surface to the divergence of the field inside the volume and it connects surface integrals to volume integrals generalizing the fundamental theorem of calculus to three dimensions

a topological space is a set together with a collection of open sets satisfying specific axioms and topology studies properties that are preserved under continuous deformations like stretching and bending but not tearing or gluing

the fourier transform decomposes a function into its constituent frequencies and is fundamental to signal processing physics and engineering allowing analysis of periodic and non periodic signals in the frequency domain

cantors diagonal argument proves that the set of real numbers is uncountably infinite meaning there is no way to list all real numbers in a sequence even though the natural numbers and rationals are both countably infinite

differential equations describe how quantities change over time and they model physical phenomena from planetary orbits to population growth with solutions that can be exact or approximated numerically
the law of large numbers states that as the number of trials increases the sample average converges to the expected value
modular arithmetic studies remainders after division and forms the basis of number theory and modern cryptography
sine and cosine are periodic functions that describe oscillation and relate angles to ratios of sides in right triangles
the fibonacci sequence starts with zero and one and each subsequent number is the sum of the two preceding numbers
mathematical induction proves statements for all natural numbers by showing the base case and that each case implies the next
the determinant of a matrix is a scalar value that indicates whether the matrix is invertible and describes how the transformation scales areas or volumes
bayes theorem describes how to update the probability of a hypothesis given new evidence using prior probability and the likelihood of the observed data
complex numbers extend the real numbers by including the imaginary unit i which satisfies i squared equals negative one
the triangle inequality states that the sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle must be greater than the length of the remaining side
a continuous function has no breaks jumps or holes in its graph and small changes in input produce small changes in output
